narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a solid sixth-decile ranking in your first-year school grades, which is slightly above average, but a lower first-decile standing in your third-year grades, alongside a middle-income family background, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 26.50, and plans to attend part-time. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 56% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed on that critical exam. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year decile ranking, which stood out as notably lower than typical students' averages and signaled potential struggles in sustaining performance over time. In contrast, your first-year decile had a meaningful positive pull, nudging the model toward seeing you as capable of passing, much like it does for students at or above average there. Your LSAT score was the next strongest influence toward failure, falling well below the dataset average and highlighting a key area where many successful bar passers excel higher. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance, leaning toward success since it's close to average, though not quite at the level of top performers. Being part-time and from a middle-income bracket provided smaller boosts toward passing, aligning with patterns where full-time students dominate but part-timers sometimes thrive with focus. Compared to typical students—who often have stronger third-year grades, higher LSATs around 37, and full-time plans—your profile tipped just over the edge into higher risk. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers, but a data-driven call based on these patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted prep or additional courses, and exploring full-time options if feasible—these steps have helped many in similar situations improve their profiles for future applications. You're resilient, and with focused effort, you can reshape this story. Keep pushing.
